coredata.dc:description: Nitrite ion (NO2 –), renowned for its potent antiseptic properties and color-preserving abilities, potentially leads to carcinogenesis and hypertension if consumed excessively. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent probe exhibits specific response to NO2 – using nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with a low detection limit (LOD) as low as 52 nM and a low quantification limit (LOQ) of 173 nM. Initially, the emission peaks at 495 nm and 415 nm decrease as result of the internal filtration effect (IFE). With further increasing NO2 – concentration, the reaction between NO2 – and N-CDs forms an azoic compound (N-CDs@NO2 –), which promotes the instant quenching of the emission peak at 415 nm through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Moreover, portable N-CDs/LFASs devices with smartphone are successfully applied to visually identify NO2 – with the exceptional LOD, achieving excellent recoveries and high precision in real sample. Therefore, this newly N-CDs/LFASs offers a reliable means for the NO2 – detection, showcasing potential applications.

head.ce:title: Lateral flow assay with dual-emission nitrogen-doped carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent determination of nitrite in food

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrite ion (NO2 –), renowned for its potent antiseptic properties and color-preserving abilities, potentially leads to carcinogenesis and hypertension if consumed excessively. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent probe exhibits specific response to NO2 – using nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with a low detection limit (LOD) as low as 52 nM and a low quantification limit (LOQ) of 173 nM. Initially, the emission peaks at 495 nm and 415 nm decrease as result of the internal filtration effect (IFE). With further increasing NO2 – concentration, the reaction between NO2 – and N-CDs forms an azoic compound (N-CDs@NO2 –), which promotes the instant quenching of the emission peak at 415 nm through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Moreover, portable N-CDs/LFASs devices with smartphone are successfully applied to visually identify NO2 – with the exceptional LOD, achieving excellent recoveries and high precision in real sample. Therefore, this newly N-CDs/LFASs offers a reliable means for the NO2 – detection, showcasing potential applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite ion (NO2 –), regarded as an inert end product of endogenous nitric oxide metabolism, is widely present in nature as simple inorganic salts [1,2]. As a food additive, its widespread application in the food processing industry owing to its ability to enhance the appearance of meat and improve flavor by reacting with myoglobin [3]. Additionally, it inhibits the growth of Clostridium botulinum, thereby extending the shelf life of meat products and enhancing the safety of edible meat products [4]. However, excessive NO2 – can adversely affect the oxygen transport function of hemoglobin, leading to methemoglobinemia due to inadequate oxygen delivery by hemoglobin [5]. Besides, NO2 – undergoes a reaction with amines, resulting in the formation of carcinogenic nitrosamines, which are associated with significant harm to human health, such as esophageal and gastrointestinal cancer [6]. Consequently, the consumption of meats containing NaNO2 due to their association between specific types of cancer lead to their classification as Group (1) carcinogens by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) [7]. The maximum permissible amount in food residue is 30 mg/kg, as per Chinese National Standards [8]. In consequence, it is of importance to devise straightforward and effective methodologies for detecting NO2 –, providing analysis paradigms for both consumers and manufacturers. Indeed, some sensitive and reliable methods have been reported for the detection of NO2 –, including surface-enhanced Raman scattering [9], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [10], chemiluminescence [11], spectrophotometry [12], and electrochemical approach [13]. Nevertheless, in comparison to the fluorescent method, majority of above methods face limitations such as high operating skill, prolonged detection time, complicated operation methods and expensive instruments. Therefore, the development of a rapid, highly selective and low-cost fluorescence probe for NO2 – analysis is of great importance.

Recently, various kinds of fluorescence sensors have been emerged for the purpose of detecting NO2 –, including polymer dots, semiconductor quantum dots, fluorescent nanoclusters, fluorescent nanodiamonds, carbon dots (CDs) and so on. Especially, sensors based on CDs have garnered significant interest owing to their high sensitivity, excellent photostability, minimal cell toxicity, superior biocompatibility, cost-effectiveness and user-friendly operation [14,15]. There are many substances that can form the precursor of synthesis, such as small aliphatic molecules, anilines, polyphenol, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, organic dyes or biomass as precursors [16–22]. Among them, the CDs obtained from anilines and polyphenol precursors can inherit and retain the long-wavelength emission and the abundant hydroxyl and amino groups, which are useful to detection of NO2 – [23]. Besides, most of these sensors use single-color “turn-off” or “turn-on” types probes for NO2 – detection. For example, Li et al. fabricated the CDs exhibiting an emission wavelength of 490 nm for NO2 – detection with the LOD of 7.1 nM due to the occurrence of photoinduced electron transfer process (PET) [24]. Hu et al. established the “turn-on” probe using CDs-based system to detect NO2 – by blocking off fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) phenomenon between neutral red and CDs [25]. Nevertheless, these single-emission fluorescent probes are susceptible to external factors such as the external environment and instruments. Ratiometric fluorescence probes possess the capability to minimize or eliminate the fluctuation in the intensity of light emission and the environmental interference. Typically, for quantitative analysis, the significant fluorescent signals are measured at distinct emission wavelengths to determine the intensity ratio. Recently, lateral flow assay strips (LFASs) have attracted more attentions as a prevalent rapid detection technology for analyzing targets related to environmental monitoring, food safety and clinical diagnosis [26]. LFASs measurement involves transferring the sample onto a paper strip, where it moves along a porous membrane due to capillary force. As the sample flow through a designated area, the desired analyte can be labelled using a signal substance such as certain fluorescent probes or colored particles [27]. This straightforward method generally demonstrates excellent analytical capabilities in relation to repeatability and specificity. Therefore, the utilization of ratiometric fluorescent probes based on CDs in combination with LFASs is of crucial importance for swift and accurate detection of NO2 –.

In this study, a ratometric fluorescence probe using nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) is constructed for the NO2 – determination in food sample. The N-CDs are prepared through a simple solvothermal treatment via catechol as oxygen source and 3, 3′-daminobenzidine as nitrogen source, which display two emission peaks at 415 nm and 495 nm with good stability (Scheme 1 ). As the concentration of NO2 – increases, the fluorescence intensities quench due to IFE between NO2 – and N-CDs. Furthermore, a diazo coupling reaction takes place between NO2 – and N-CDs, forming the azoic compound (N-CDs@NO2 –), which results in further additional suppression of the emission peak at 415 nm through FRET. By recording the I 415/I 495 (the fluorescence intensity ratio between the emission peaks at 415 nm and 495 nm), the ratiometric probe exhibits remarkable sensitivity and selectivity toward NO2 – with a low detection limit (LOD) of 52 nM and a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 173 nM from 0.052 to 100 µM. Additionally, a real-time NO2 – detection device is constructed using the LFASs and N-CDs in food samples, in which the color channel values can be determined by a smartphone with a LOD of 156 nM. More importantly, this device achieves good quantitative analysis results in real samples with admirable percentage recoveries of 98.4–105.1 % and relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 1.13–2.29 %. In conclusion, the novel analytical strategy expands the potential applications of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor in the determination of NO2 – and offers a new avenue on monitoring of NO2 – in foods.

All chemicals and reagents utilized in the subsequent experiments were possessed the highest purity or analytical grade without further purification. Catechol (99.0 %), 3, 3′-daminobenzidine (99.0 %), NaNO2 (99.0 %), ethanol (>99.9 %), NaCl (99.9 %), KCl (99.9 %), ZnCl2 (99.9 %), MgSO4 (99.9 %), BaCl2 (99.9 %), NiCl2 (99.9 %), CdCl2 (98.0 %), CoCl2 (99.9 %), AgCl (99.9 %), CaCl2 (99.8 %), KI (99.9 %), other anionic solution, glutathione (98.0 %), arginine (98.0 %), threonine (99.0 %), L-methionine (99.0 %), DL-methionine (99.8 %), glycine (99.0 %), histidine (98.0 %) and alanine (99.0 %) were purchased by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). The procurement of starch and nitrocellulose (NC) membranes were facilitated through Millipore Corporation (Bedford, MA, USA). The polyvinyl chloride (PVC) (99.8 %) sheets, sample pad, conjugate pad and absorbent pad were sourced from Kinbio Tech Company (Shanghai, China). The deionized water (>18.0 MΩ∙cm), obtained from the Millipore system, was used throughout all experiments. The samples (bacon, sausage, pickle and salted fish) were purchased from a local supermarket (Ningbo, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of N-CDs were obtained by utilizing the Model JEM-2100F (Electron Optics Laboratory Ltd, Japan). Concurrently, atomic force microscope (AFM, Oxford Instruments, Cypher S) was employed to investigate the morphology and size distribution of the N-CDs. Raman measurements were performed by HR800 Evolution instrument from HORIBA Jobin Yvon. The fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs both in the presence and absence of NO2 – were identified by PerkinElmer Instruments with the excitation wavelength of 360 nm under room temperature. Similarly, X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Agilent FT-IR spectrometer) were employed for the analysis of the elements and functional groups. Fluorescence measurements including photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra, along with the Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were performed thought a PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer and a UV-5800 spectrophotometer. All fluorescence images were captured using a Xiaomi 14 smartphone under a portable ultraviolet lamp (AGL-9406, 365 nm). The Agilent 1260 system, equipped with the ultraviolet (UV) detector (268 nm) was utilized for HPLC analysis.

N-CDs were synthesized according to using catechol as oxygen source and 3, 3′-diaminobenzidine as nitrogen source, following a modified solvothermal method [28,29]. In brief, a mixture of 1 mmol of catechol and 0.5 mmol of 3, 3′-diaminobenzidine in 60 mL of ethanol was transferred to a Teflon autoclave liner which are reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. The mixture was naturally cooled down to room temperature. Next, the N-CDs were collected by centrifugation at a speed of 7000 rpm for 0.5 h to eliminate larger particles through, followed by dialysis using a membrane with molecular weight cutoff ranging from 500 to 1000 Da for 24 h. Finally, the purified solution was frozen at −40 °C overnight and then lyophilized using a bench top freeze dryer (SCIENTZ-100F, Scientz) under condenser temperature −70 °C, pressure 10 Pa condition. The processing time was 36 h, and the N-CDs solids were obtained for the further use.

The fluorescence quantum yield represents the efficiency of transforming absorbed photons into emitted photons. To determine the relative QY of the unknown sample, we could refer to the known emission and absorption characteristics of well-established QY standards, such as rhodamine 6G. A meticulous preparation was carried out to create a series of N-CDs solutions with varying concentrations, ensuring that the optical absorbance values for each solution remained below 0.10. Subsequently, the QY of N-CDs was determined with the equation [30]:

where F represents the emission intensity integral, A refers the absorbance and η presents the refractive index of the solution (1.36 for ethanol). The subscript “X” signifies the sample to be tested (N-CDs), while “ST” means the standard (rhodamine 6G).

In summary, 200 μL of NO2 – solution with concentration range from 0 to 500 µM and 100 μL of N-CDs were sequentially introduced into the microtubes, which were diluted to a final volume of 3 mL using with Britton-Robinson buffer (pH=7). Then, after incubated at room temperature for 2 min, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was measured with 360 nm excitation wavelength. To investigate the selectivity of N-CDs towards NO2 –, a range of different metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, K+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+), anions (Br-, I-, Cl-, F-, HCO3 –, CO3 2–, SCN-, CNS-, PO4 3-, SO4 2-, NO3 –) and amino acids (glutathione, arginine, threonine, L-methionine, DL-methionine, glycine, histidine, alanine) were individually added to the solution, then measuring their fluorescence spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of LFASs and test procedure
ce:section.ce:para: The LFASs were composed of three pads, namely absorbent pad, conjugate pad and sample pad, along with an NC membrane. Briefly, the starch (1 mg/mL) and the N-CDs (1 mg/mL) were coated onto the NC membrane as control line (C-line) and test line (T-line) at a scribing speed of 1 μL/cm, respectively. Subsequently, each component was attached to the PVA sheet with a 2-mm overlap between two components. After drying the pads in an oven at 37 °C for 2 h, the obtained pads were cut into strips with a width of 3.5 mm using a CM 4000 cutter to form the LFASs loaded with N-CDs (N-CDs/LFASs). Ultimately, the assembled N-CDs/LFASs were securely enclosed within a dry environment using a plastic bag.

A range of NO2 – standard solution with concentrations (0–500 μM) were added onto the sample pad (totaling a volume of 100 μL), and the conjugate pad was enriched with KI (1 mg/mL). The detection procedure was carried out in the dark, and the outcomes were obtained within 8 min. Afterwards, the N-CDs/LFASs test results were observed by naked eyes (C-line) and recorded the RGB (Red, Green, Blue) values (T-line) using smartphone software (Picker) under the ultraviolet lamp at 360 nm. After photographing the N-CDs/LFASs, a calibration curve was established for the quantitative NO2 – detection with each test performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CDs/LFASs in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: Real samples (bacon, sausage, pickle and salted fish) were accurately weighed (each weighing 2.5 g), fragmented using a blender and individually submerged in 80 mL of water. After undergoing 15 min of ultrasonic treatment and 10 min of heating in a beaker at 70 °C, the samples were allowed to cool down to room temperature. Subsequently, each sample was modified to a volume of 50 mL. The filtrate was collected for further analysis after passing through a 0.22 μm pore-sized filter membrane. Eventually, the detection of NO2 – used the N-CDs/LFASs in real samples according to the above-mentioned test procedure. The experiment was conducted on HPLC as a reference method, and the resulting t-test was analyzed. All experiments are conducted with triple parallel tests to increase the reliability of the experiment.

A series of characterization methods are unitized to explore the structural, particle size and morphological of N-CDs. There is no apparent aggregation in the solution, and N-CDs are evenly distributed throughout, as depicted in Fig. 1
(a). The HRTEM image of N-CDs reveals a distinct and clear crystal lattice around 0.21 nm (inset in Fig. 1 (a)), suggesting the presence of the (100) facet with graphene [31]. Fig. 1 (b) shows the dispersion of N-CDs within an average diameter-range of 2.1–6.4 nm and a mean diameter of 3.9 nm, aligning well with the HRTEM finding. Meanwhile, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement ( Fig. S1 ) reveals an average hydrodynamic size of 4.07 nm, which is in line with the result of TEM analysis. As outlined in Fig. S2 , the thickness of N-CDs, as observed from the AFM image and represented by a height profile along the white line, ranges between 0.39 nm and 1.68 nm [32]. In addition, a major diffusive peak is observed at around 23.09° in N-CDs ( Fig. S3 ), indicating the existence of a graphite-like arrangement. More importantly, the Raman spectrum typically exhibits a pair of peaks at 1519 cm−1 and 1562 cm−1, which representing the disordered arrangements (D band) and graphitic carbon regions (G band) ( Fig. S4 ), respectively. The ratio I D/I G of 0.698 implies a high graphitization level of N-CDs, which corresponds to the distinct crystallinity degree [33]. The chemical structures of N-CDs are additionally investigated utilizing a FT-IR and XPS spectrometer. The FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs exhibits a significantly strong and wide absorption band at 2628–3444 cm−1, corresponding to stretching vibrations of C-H and O-H/N-H, as depicted in Fig. 1 (c). The existence of four absorption peaks at 1463 cm−1, 1252 cm−1, 1012 cm−1 and 917 cm−1 are attributed to the covalent of C=O, C=N/C-H, O-H and C-O bonds [34], respectively. The above results demonstrate the presence of –COOH, –OH and –NH2 groups on the surface of N-CDs [35]. Moreover, the XPS analysis provides confirmation of the chemical valence states and the elemental composition of the N-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. S5 , the surface of N-CDs contains three elements, including C (70.57 %), N (16.33 %) and O (13.10 %) ( Fig. S6 ), with homologous peaks observed at 282.37 eV, 399.31 eV, and 531.55 eV [36]. To provide more specificity, Fig. 1 (d) shows that the groups C=O/C=N, C-N/C-O and C=C/C-C are attribute to three peaks at 285.24 eV, 284.42 eV and 283.41 eV in the high resolution C 1 s spectrum [37]. The deconvolution of high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1 (e)) describes three distinct bond types including N-H (399.9 eV), N-C (397.8 eV) and N=C (399.1 eV) [38]. Furthermore, the binding energy of the O 1 s peaks at 532.1 eV and 530.5 eV are the presence of C-O and C=O (Fig. 1 (f)) [39], respectively. All in all, the FT-IR and XPS spectra exhibit well consistent, confirming the existence of numerous functional groups on the surface of N-CDs.

The UV–vis absorption, PL and PLE spectra of N-CDs solution are evaluated through measurements at room temperature to assess their optical properties. As depicted in Fig. 1 (g), an obvious peak at 225 nm and a faint peak at 291 nm illustrate the presence of the π-π* transition of C=C and the n-π* transition of C=O/C=N in the UV–vis spectrum of N-CDs [40]. Moreover, upon excitation with 360 nm radiation, N-CDs display two distinct emission peaks at 415 nm and 495 nm. The provided illustration reveals that N-CDs exhibit a subtle yellow in natural light and a cyan glow under ultraviolet light. As illustrated in Fig. 1 (h), the emission spectra under various excitation wavelengths (300–410 nm) suggest that the fluorescence of N-CDs is mainly attributed to functional surface chemical groups [41]. Furthermore, the 3D mapping of PLE and PL wavelength reveals the existence of two emission centers, exhibiting two emission peaks at 415 nm and 495 nm (Fig. 1 (i)). Furthermore, the QYs of N-CDs are determined to be 24 % and 27 %, which are in comparison to other doped CDs ( Table S1 ). Given the importance of optical stability as a fluorescent probe, the fluorescent performances of N-CDs are evaluated under various experimental conditions. The PL intensity is almost unchanged over a 24-hour ultraviolet irradiation period, as shown in Fig. S7
(a). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity remains stable after 30 days of storage at room temperature, thereby indicating good storage stability ( Fig. S7
(b)). Moreover, Fig. S7
(c) demonstrates that the fluorescence intensities remain constant even when the NaCl concentration reaches 2.0 M in the solution, indicating exceptional resistance to salt. In summary, the above findings suggest that brilliant fluorescence stability of N-CDs is suitable for the NO2 – detection.

As we all know, the different experimental conditions greatly influence the detection performances of N-CDs. Therefore, sensor conditions, including reaction temperature, pH values and incubation time, have been meticulously optimized before NO2 – detection. Specifically, the evaluation of the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the N-CDs across a temperature range from 15 to 55 °C enables determination of the optimum temperature. The intensity ratio I 415/I 495 is chosen as the detection response value. The results show that the optimal temperature, with the least influence on fluorescence intensity, occurs at room temperature, as depicted in Fig. 2
(a). The I 415/I 495 value exhibits a gradual increase with the rise in the pH from 3 to 6, attains the optimum at pH=7, and then decline sharply as the pH value rises from 8 to 13 (Fig. 2 (b)). The acidic and alkaline media may cause damage to the surface states of the N-CDs, impacting the interaction between NO2 – and N-CDs. Consequently, pH=7 is selected as the optimal value for NO2 – analysis. Moreover, Fig. 2 (c) describes the fluctuation of the intensity ratio I 415/I 495 under different incubation times. The N-CDs exhibit a stable fluorescence intensity within 2 min, demonstrating the system achieves equilibrium instantaneously and possesses quick response capability. Therefore, to minimize experimental errors, 2 min is preferred as the standard incubation time. In general, the above experiment conditions are advantageous for the real-time detection of NO2 –.

Using the optimal measurement conditions, the potential of utilizing N-CDs fluorescence sensor to determine NO2 – is further evaluated. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs exhibits a progressive decrease as the NO2 – concentration increasing, and the decrease of the emission peaks at 415 nm is much greater than that of the one at 495 nm, as recorded in Fig. 2 (d). The ratiometric fluorescence intensity demonstrates a favorable linear correlation with the concentration of NO2 – across two different ranges (Fig. 2 (e)). This discrepancy in slope may be attributed to variations in the different quenching mechanisms. As shown in Fig. 2 (f), the equation of linear regression shows Y=-0.00292X+0.87782 (R 2 = 0.99202) at NO2 – concentrations ranging from 0.052 to 100 µM, where Y is the I 415/I 495 of reaction mixture, and X represents the NO2 – concentration. In addition, the target LOD and LOQ are exceptionally low at 52 nM and 173 nM, based on LOD=3σ/S and LOQ=10σ/S, where σ and S are the standard deviation and the slope of the linear calibration line. Importantly, in contrast with other previously reported detection methods, this ratiometric fluorescence probe utilizing N-CDs exhibits lower LOD and LOQ ( Table S2 and Fig. S8 ). Thus, the above findings demonstrate the considerable potential of N-CDs for NO2 – detection through ratiometric fluorescence.

As ratiometric fluorescent sensors, the selectivity of N-CDs is demonstrated under the same conditions with the existence of various interfering substances, such as different metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, K+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+), anions (Br-, I-, Cl-, F-, HCO3 –, CO3 2–, SCN-, CNS-, PO4 3-, SO4 2-, NO3 –) and amino acids (glutathione, arginine, threonine, L-methionine, DL-methionine, glycine, histidine, alanine). As demonstrated in Fig. 2 (g), only N-CDs with NO2 – and Fe3+ induce the PL changes, meanwhile others have minimal impact under the optimal excitation. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. S9 , the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs exhibits a progressive decrease as the NO2 – concentration increasing, and the decrease of the emission peaks at 415 nm is much greater than that of the one at 495 nm, while the Fe3+ shows an inverse outcome. Based on this, the established fluorescence probe (I 495/I 415) still has a good selectivity for NO2 –. Thus, most ions can coexist in the detection of NO2 – without affecting the overall detection process. Besides, the N-CDs solution with the addition of NO2 – no longer emits cyan, regardless of the presence or absence of other ions, under the ultraviolet light ( Fig. S10 ). These results indicate that the probe shows favorable analytical abilities and is suitable for practical applications in the NO2 – determination during real food samples analysis.

Up to now, sensing mechanisms using fluorescence of N-CDs include electronic energy transfer (EET), PET, FRET, intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), IFE, static quenching and dynamic quenching [42]. For elucidating the detection mechanism of N-CDs to NO2 –, various analyses are conducted, including the zeta potential, UV–vis absorption, XPS analysis and FT-IR spectra. A noticeable change undergoes in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs following the introduction of NO2 –, as depicted in Fig. 3
(a). The new peak at 438 nm emerges after incubation with NO2 –, indicating the presence of coordination interaction between NO2 – and surface groups of N-CDs [43]. At the same time, Fig. S11 shows that the zeta potential of NO2 – is performed to −8.12 mV, while the zeta potentials of N-CDs with and without NO2 – change from 2.12 to 17.54 mV, respectively. This significant shift of zeta potential indicates strong interactions between N-CDs and NO2 – [44]. The Benesi-Hildebrand equation is employed to calculate the binding constant (K BH), which reflects the interaction strength and further confirms the binding between NO2 – and N-CDs [45]:

F 0
where F 0 and F stand fluorescent intensities of N-CDs without and with analyte, respectively. F s is the fluorescence intensity at saturated analyte concentration, while C corresponds to the analyte concentration. As shown in Fig. S12 , the K BH value reaches up to 7.02 × 104 mol−1, revealing a strong attraction between NO2 – and N-CDs. Based on these results, we speculate that this newly formed substance may arise from the interaction occurring between NO2 – and the amino group within N-CDs [46]. Furthermore, in contrast to the initial spectrum of N-CDs, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s for N-CDs with NO2 – reveals the presence of a new peak at 398.0 eV corresponding to −N=N- (Fig. 3 (b)), suggesting successful diazotization between the NO2 – and N-CDs [47]. Simultaneously, the FT-IR spectra of N-CDs are also examined in the presence and absence of NO2 –. Interestingly, a novel peak at 1737 cm−1 is attributed to the −N=N-, while the amine peaks at 2628 cm−1 have nearly disappeared (Fig. 3 (c)) [48]. Hence, the azoic compound (N-CDs@NO2 –) is generated via the diazotization process involving primary arylamines. Specifically, when the amine surface of N-CDs interacts with NO2 –, both NO2 – and N-CDs@NO2 – concurrently coexist within the solution.

Additionally, the absorption spectra of NO2 – and N-CDs@NO2 – significantly overlap with the PLE and PL spectra of N-CDs within the range of 250–440 nm, which indicate that the detection quenching mechanism may involve IFE and FRET (Fig. 3 (d)) [49]. Concretely speaking, the wide absorption peak at 350 nm of NO2 – coincides with the excitation peak of N-CDs, indicating a potential quenching mechanism for the dual fluorescence emission peaks due to IFE. Simultaneously, as the concentration of NO2 – continuously increases, a portion of NO2 – undergoes diazotized reaction with N-CDs, forming N-CDs@NO2 –. Therefore, the emerging absorption peak at 430 nm of N-CDs@NO2 – overlaps with the emission peak at 415 nm of N-CDs, expediting the detection process, which is consistent with the actual measurement. As we know, the fluorescence lifetime of a fluorophore remains constant during IFE, while fluorescence lifetime changes during FRET [50]. As portrayed in Table S3 and Fig. 3 (e), the fluorescence lifetimes of the emission (415 nm) display a negligible change (from 1.58 to 1.50 ns) and an apparent change (from 1.58 to 1.09 ns) after adding microscale (10 µM) and sufficient (100 µM) NO2 –, respectively. This further certifies that the PL emission quenching mechanism involves IFE and FRET, consistent with our previous discussion. In relation to the emission peak observed at 495 nm, inconspicuous changes in the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs indicate that the fluorescence quenching mechanism is IFE.

In addition to that, we also perform Parker equation to enhance the verification of the IFE and FTRE mechanism. According the previous studies, Parker equation is a quantitative analytical method to demonstrate the IFE [51,52]:

where F obsd represents the measured fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 495 nm or 415 nm; F cor refers to the corrected fluorescence intensity obtained by subtracting IFE from F obsd; A ex denotes the absorbance measurement at 360 nm, which is the excitation wavelength for the N-CDs and A em corresponds to the emission wavelengths at 415 nm and 495 nm. The thickness of excitation beam (l) is set at 0.10 cm and h refers to the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette (0.40 cm), while s denotes the width of cuvette (1.00 cm). The calculated corrected and measured quench efficiencies (E cor and E obsd) are derived from the corrected and measured fluorescence intensities (F cor and F obsd) [53]. Table S4 and Table S5 summarize the calculated results before and after the addition of NO2 – at 415 nm and 495 nm, respectively. The E cor and E obsd values of the N-CDs at the 415 nm are suppressed to 63.5 % and 35.2 %, as shown in Fig. 3 (f), implying the presence of another quenching mechanism alongside IFE. Building upon these findings, the quenching mechanism of the emission at 415 nm involves the IFE between N-CDs and NO2 –, as well as FRET between N-CDs@NO2 – and N-CDs. Furthermore, with the increased NO2 – concentration, the suppressed efficiencies about 495 nm of the observed (49.4 %) and corrected fluorescence (15.5 %) are exhibited in Fig. S13 , further confirming the existence of the IFE mechanism and ruling out the other mechanism [54]. All in all, these results indicate that the synergistic IFE and FRET effect contributes to the NO2 – detection using N-CDs, and Fig. 3 (g) shows the corresponding schematic diagram of quenching mechanism.

The N-CDs/LFASs system comprises NC membrane containing T-line and C-line, absorbent pad, sample pad and conjugate pad, as shown in Fig. 4
(a). In order to enhance the analytical efficiency of this LFASs, various experimental factors are optimized, such as incubation time, N-CDs concentration, and dosages of KI and starch. To determine the optimal incubation time on the test strips, the G/B value of the T-line is evaluated through fluorescence analysis. Test strips photos are taken, and Fig. S14
(a) shows that a stable signal is achieved after 8 min. As depicted in Fig. S14
(b), varying concentrations of N-CDs (0.50–1.50 mg/mL) are used and labeled with the NO2 – to obtain a stable detection process. The fluorescence intensities increase with concentrations ranging from 0.50 to 1.0 mg/mL, reaching a plateau and remaining stable thereafter. Therefore, the optimal concentration is determined to be 1.0 mg/ml as it yields the highest test line signal strength. Besides, different dosages of KI and starch (from 0.25 to 1.75 μL) are applied to coat the samples pad and C-line on the NC membrane. It is observed that the C-line band exhibits optimal intensity when the dosages of KI and starch are set at 1.0 μL ( Fig. S14
(c)).

When the sample solutions flow through the NC membrane, the C-line becomes visible in daylight, while the T-line is observable using the UV light at 360 nm. To ensure the validity of the T-line, the C-line must always appear blue due to the reaction between KI and starch (Fig. 4 (b)). The overall detection process involves the sample acquisition, image capture and RBG analysis with the smartphone‐based application, as depicted in Fig. 4 (c). Specifically, NO2 – standard solutions at various concentrations are tested using the N-CDs/LFASs in triplicate (Fig. 4 (d)). Clearly, the cyan fluorescence from T-line exhibits a gradual decline trend until it disappears along with increasing NO2 – concentrations. Moreover, the RGB values of T-line are recorded using a smartphone app (Color Picker) during testing. To further improve the accuracy of detection, NO2 – concentration and the ratio of G/B serves as parameters for establishing an internal relationship (G/B=0.00074C0 + 0.98835), yielding a LOD of 156 nM (Fig. 4 (e-f)). These results indicate that the N-CDs/LFASs sensing device holds promise as a visual and rapid method for NO2 – detection.

Apart from sensitivity, the selectivity relies another crucial factor for assessing the performance of N-CDs/LFASs in target detection. For assessing the selectivity of N-CDs/LFASs for NO2 –, the T-line against interferences from different metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, K+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+), anions (Br-, I-, Cl-, F-, HCO3 –, CO3 2–, SCN-, CNS-, PO4 3-, SO4 2-, NO3 –) and amino acids (glutathione, arginine, threonine, L-methionine, DL-methionine, glycine, histidine, alanine) at the concentration of 50 μM are tested (Fig. 4 (g) and Fig. S15 ). As previously established, it is evident that T-line of the N-CDs/LFASs experiences reduction fluorescence intensity solely in the presence of NO2 –, whereas unnoticeable alterations in fluorescence quenching are observed with the other metal ions and anions. These findings suggest that our detection system exhibits strong analytical abilities and holds promise for the assessment NO2 – in real food samples.

To demonstrate the effectiveness and reliability of N-CDs/LFASs, bacon, pickle, sausage and salted fish are collected and analyzed as actual samples. The HPLC analysis is selected as the reference method. The test flow is recorded in Video S1 , revealing significant changes in the T-line of N-CDs/LFASs under ultraviolet light after adding the sample (Fig. 4 (h)). As indicated in Table S6 and Fig. S16 , the spiked recoveries fall ranging from 98.4 % to 105.1 %, and the RSDs are less than 2.29 %. These results indicate the high accuracy of the proposed method. Furthermore, the HPLC method yielded consistent analysis outcomes with the proposed approach, providing unambiguous evidence that N-CDs/LFASs are highly accurate for determining NO2 – in real samples. In brief, these results provide evidence of the selectivity and sensitivity of N-CDs/LFASs device in identifying NO2 – in actual samples.

In summary, an efficient and ultrasensitive fluorescence probe is developed for the rapid determination of NO2 – using N-CDs. These carbon dots are successfully prepared in a one-step solvothermal process, employing 3, 3′-daminobenzidine as oxygen source and catechol as nitrogen source, respectively. Briefly, the fluorescence intensity at 415 nm undergoes a significant decrease due to the impact of IFE between N-CDs and NO2 –, along with the FRET between N-CDs and N-CDs@NO2 –. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity (495 nm) experiences a gradual decrease owing to the influence of IFE caused by NO2 –. The ratometric probe, as characterized by the I 415/I 495 values, exhibits excellent selectivity and sensitivity and toward NO2 – within a LOD of 52 nM and a LOQ of 173 nM within the range from 0.052 to 100 µM. Additionally, we combine this fluorescence probe with LFASs to realize convenient NO2 – detection. The N-CDs/LFASs, functioning as a tool for field-testing and integrating multiple technologies, effectively distinguishes the NO2 – content in real food samples, yielding satisfactory recovery rate from 98.4 % to 105.1 % and RSD less than 2.29 %. This innovative analysis strategy not only expands the horizons for ratiometric fluorescence sensor but also paves the way for a new approach to monitoring NO2 – in foods.
